title: Fifty-One Tales
description: Fifty-One Tales — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Fifty-One Tales" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: A Losing Game
memes.description: Man encounters Death in a tavern and tries to cheer his defeated opponent — only to realize that Death's grief may be for the end of the game itself rather than for any particular loss.

memes.label: A Losing Game
memes.description: "A Losing Game" is a tale in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales that presents an event of futile contest — a game that cannot be won.

memes.label: A Mistaken Identity
memes.description: Fame encounters Notoriety flaunting beneath a gas-lamp, and when the imposter claims Fame's name, the true Fame quietly withdraws — leaving Notoriety to lead her worshippers where they belong.

memes.label: A Moral Little Tale
memes.description: A single-line tale whose title announces a moral fable but whose body never appears — a meta-fictional jest about the reader's expectation that stories must teach lessons.

memes.label: After the Fire
memes.description: After the world collides with a black uncharted star, alien beings sift through the cinders searching for what was greatest — and discover it was not the mammoth.

memes.label: After the Fire
memes.description: "After the Fire" is a tale in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales that portrays the aftermath of a great conflagration — an event of destruction and the silence that follows.

memes.label: Alone the Immortals
memes.description: The narrator travels by dream to a valley where dead years are said to lie, bearing a daisy wreath as mourning — only to discover that the dead years are not frail ghosts but colossal immortals before whom Time itself kneels in vassalage.

memes.label: Angel
memes.description: The angel as divine builder and instrument of judgment in Fifty-One Tales — appearing as the architect of a modern Hell, the cosmic surveyor in "The Worm and the Angel," and the sculpted conqueror on Pan's tomb.

memes.label: Athene
memes.description: The Greek goddess of wisdom and war, invoked in a single passing reference in "Death and Odysseus" as knowing the hall in Ithaca to which Odysseus returns.

memes.label: Babylon
memes.description: The ancient Mesopotamian city, appearing in the Fifty-One Tales as an emblem of fallen civilizations — its wreckage floats on Oblivion's sea, its winged bulls slain by time.

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Beauty in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — a force that captivates Time himself, inspires the Blackbird's song, and survives in the rosy marble of a dead god, yet is never separable from the mortality of the things that bear it.

memes.label: Caucasus
memes.description: The mountain range between Europe and Asia, appearing in "The Unpasturable Fields" as a point of comparison for the mountains' majesty.

memes.label: Index of Characters
memes.description: Complete index of all characters, mythological figures, and personified entities appearing in Fifty-One Tales.

memes.label: Charon
memes.description: A brief prose poem in which Charon, the ferryman of the dead, rows eternally — the tale explores the weight of eternal labor, the indifference of the cosmos, and the coming of the last soul.

memes.label: Charon
memes.description: The mythological ferryman of the dead, portrayed by Dunsany as a weary, dutiful figure who rows souls across the Styx without pondering their origins or destinations — until a single, mysterious passenger makes him smile.

memes.label: Compromise
memes.description: A city of marble and gold is built atop an earthquake's lair — and when the earthquake stirs, a compromise is reached that spares the city but at a cost no one foresaw.

memes.label: Index of Concepts
memes.description: Complete index of abstract concepts, themes, and personified ideas explored across Fifty-One Tales.

memes.label: Dawn
memes.description: Dawn and morning appear as recurring motifs in Fifty-One Tales, associated with renewal, the larks' song, and the passage of time across the worlds.

memes.label: Death and Odysseus
memes.description: A tale in which Odysseus, the crafty Greek hero, encounters Death personified and — rather than fleeing — engages him in conversation, turning an appointed doom into an unexpected alliance.

memes.label: Death and the Orange
memes.description: Two young men and one woman sit at a restaurant table in a southern land; a small orange with an evil laugh rolls to the floor, and Death — already dining at another table — rises to listen to the quarrel.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The personification of death in Lord Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales, appearing as a complex figure who is alternately gloomy, weeping, and vulnerable.

memes.label: Doom
memes.description: Doom as an inescapable force in Fifty-One Tales — the preordained destruction that hangs over cities, souls, and civilizations, woven through tales of prophecy and cosmic inevitability.

memes.label: Dream
memes.description: Dream in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — a recurrent state that blurs the boundary between vision and reality, aspiration and mockery, the dreamed-of and the fever-dream.

memes.label: Index of Events
memes.description: Complete index of narrative events — the key story moments that structure the tales in Fifty-One Tales.

memes.label: Fame
memes.description: The personification of Fame in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — a fickle, alluring figure courted by poets and shadowed by her vulgar double, Notoriety.

memes.label: Fame (concept)
memes.description: The concept of Fame in Dunsany's tales — examined as a theme of artistic recognition, the vanity of renown, and the confusion between true achievement and mere notoriety.

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: Fate in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — a cosmic geography from whose hills the river of Time flows, carrying both civilizations and their refuse toward an inevitable cleansing flood.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: Fear as a pervasive emotional force in Fifty-One Tales — the fear of cosmic annihilation, industrial desolation, dream-death, and the loss of beauty, woven through tales of mortality and the unknown.

relations.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana

memes.label: Furrow-Maker
memes.description: Two rooks — one black, one brown — discuss the fate of Man, whom they call Furrow-Maker, debating whether his cities and engines will kill him or whether the ancient partnership between bird and ploughman will endure.

memes.label: Giza
memes.description: The site of the Great Sphinx in Egypt, appearing in "The Sphinx at Giza" (titled "The Sphinx at Gizeh") where the Sphinx paints her face to ogle Time.

memes.label: Glory
memes.description: Glory in Fifty-One Tales as the crowning achievement of civilization — the magnificent cities, ramparts, and works that Man raises as monuments to his own greatness, yet which remain vulnerable to forces beyond human control.

memes.label: God
memes.description: The gods as distant, indifferent forces in the cosmic order of Fifty-One Tales, portrayed through Charon's resignation, the personification of Death, and the capricious divine machinery that governs mortal fate.

memes.label: Himalaya
memes.description: The Himalayan mountain range, appearing in "The Unpasturable Fields" as the eastern exemplar of mountain grandeur in the clouds' display.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: Hope appears in Fifty-One Tales as a fragile human expectation, often contrasted with the eternal perspective of cosmic forces like Time and Death.

memes.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana
memes.description: A tale of a dark citadel of wizardry whose prophesied doom arrives not as a fearsome enemy but as laughter — a man from a nearby cottage who looks behind the final curtain and sees the inner mystery, ending Thlunrana's reign of terror forever.

memes.label: Immortality
memes.description: Immortality in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — the state that past years achieve as colossal entities before whom even Time kneels, and the ambiguous hope that drives both the workman's scratched name and Homer's floating raft.

memes.label: Ingra
memes.description: The joyous city in the Demagogue's vision, where the people dance and the Demi-mondaine's name will be sung long after.

memes.label: Inspiration
memes.description: Inspiration in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — the involuntary creative impulse that arises from beauty witnessed, the source of the Blackbird's song and the Poet's devotion.

memes.label: Ithaca
memes.description: The island kingdom of Odysseus in Greek myth, appearing in "Death and Odysseus" where Death arrives at Athene's hall to frighten the hero.

memes.label: Joy
memes.description: Joy as a recurring theme in the Fifty-One Tales — fleeting delight, the joy of beauty, civic joy, and the joy that persists despite and alongside mortality.

memes.label: King Karna-Vootra
memes.description: King Karna-Vootra is a monarch who, sitting on his throne commanding all things, recounts a dream vision of the queenly Vava-Nyria in the gardens of Istrakhan, a city shut in by mountains older than the moon.

memes.label: Laughter
memes.description: In Dunsany's "How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana," Laughter is the prophesied enemy — a force "of the gods though he dwelt with men" — whose arrival destroys the dark wizardry of Thlunrana.

relations.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana

memes.label: Leafy Green Street
memes.description: A London street that is the setting for "The Trouble in Leafy Green Street," where a woman buys a grey-stone idol and brings down an unspecified doom.

relations.label: The Trouble in Leafy Green Street

memes.label: Light
memes.description: Light as a symbolic force in Fifty-One Tales — the radiance absent from Charon's underworld, the moonlight of fading fairy realms, the cosmic sunlight of mountain panoramas, and the twilight that marks the boundary between the known and the mysterious.

memes.label: Lobster Salad
memes.description: In a surreal dream-narrative, the narrator climbs the sheer face of the Palace of Colquonhombros while sable apes pursue — and saves himself not by climbing further but by remembering a pin left on his bedside chest of drawers in the waking world.

memes.label: London
memes.description: The modern city that serves as a backdrop and counterpoint to mythic and pastoral settings in several tales.

memes.label: Lord Dunsany
memes.description: Lord Dunsany (Edward John Moreton Drax Plunkett, 1878–1957), 18th Baron of Dunsany, was an Irish writer and dramatist whose 1915 collection *Fifty-One Tales* pioneered the modern fantasy short story, influencing generations of writers from H.P. Lovecraft to Jorge Luis Borges.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: Love as a recurring theme in the Fifty-One Tales — romantic desire, divine affection, love of place, love as remembrance, and cosmic love as forces that shape human fate.

memes.label: Man
memes.description: A personified collective figure in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales, representing all humanity in its encounters with Death, Time, and the limits of civilization.

memes.label: Moleshill Street
memes.description: A London street where an old man keeps an idol-shop, appearing in "The Trouble in Leafy Green Street" as the source of the grey-stone god of Rainy Cheerfulness.

relations.label: The Trouble in Leafy Green Street

memes.label: Music
memes.description: Music, song, and singing recur throughout Fifty-One Tales as expressions of divine inspiration, natural beauty, and the human attempt to capture what is eternal.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: Nature in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales, depicted as a personified ancient force — a mother of wild things who comes begging at the doors of civilization.

memes.label: Nature and Time
memes.description: Through the winter streets of Coventry strides a triumphant Time, while behind him Nature — ragged, weeping, reproachful — struggles to keep pace, lamenting her dying woods, her fallen anemones, and the relentless growth of cities.

memes.label: Night
memes.description: Night as a recurring setting and symbol in the Fifty-One Tales — a time of mystery, transformation, encounter with the supernatural, and the threshold between the visible and the hidden.

memes.label: Nineveh
memes.description: The ancient Assyrian capital, appearing alongside Babylon as an emblem of fallen civilization — its wreckage drifts on Oblivion's sea.

memes.label: Nirid
memes.description: The mountain monasteries at the desert's edge in the Demagogue's vision, where the heralds' journey ends among lonely men.

memes.label: Odysseus
memes.description: The legendary Greek hero, appearing in Dunsany's "Death and Odysseus" — an aged, weary wanderer who confronts Death in his doorway and bargains for more time.

memes.label: Olympus
memes.description: The home of the Greek gods, appearing in "Death and Odysseus" as the Olympian courts where Love laughs at Death.

memes.label: Parnassus
memes.description: The mountain of the Muses in Greek myth, appearing in "The Messengers" where a hare-hunter is summoned by the Muses to carry a message.

memes.label: Pegasus
memes.description: The winged horse of Greek mythology, evoked in Dunsany's tales as a figure of transcendent beauty — a creature of the open fields and sky whose presence contrasts with the confined, earthbound world of human affairs.

memes.label: Persepolis
memes.description: The ancient Persian ceremonial capital, appearing in the Fifty-One Tales as one of the great cities destroyed by time and now darkening Oblivion's sea.

memes.label: Picadilly
memes.description: A London street that becomes the focus of a surreal tale in which workmen dismantle it to reveal the southern stars beneath.

memes.label: Pity
memes.description: Pity as a transformative force in Fifty-One Tales — the compassion that moves even Death to tears and that mortals invoke when pleading for the preservation of beauty in an indifferent cosmos.

memes.label: Index of Places
memes.description: Complete index of all real and mythical locations that serve as settings in Fifty-One Tales.

memes.label: Poetry
memes.description: Poetry in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales — the art that survives when civilizations drown, the raft that Homer built and that still floats after three thousand years.

memes.label: Roses
memes.description: A brief meditation on a roadside where wild roses bloom with strange abundance, their deeper color the sole surviving memory of a vanished village — and a hope that when London too passes, something beautiful will remain.

memes.label: Sendara
memes.description: A pastoral land in the Demagogue's vision, where men herd brown sheep and hear tidings of beauty carried by heralds.

memes.label: Song
memes.description: Song as an expression of beauty, devotion, and the human spirit in Fifty-One Tales — appearing as the poet's offering, Arcadian pastoral idyll, a binding force against divine loss, and the torment of the excluded in hell.

memes.label: Sooma
memes.description: A golden city in the Demagogue's vision, where kings sit in a lofty alabaster house and merchants carry tales to distant markets.

memes.label: Sorrow
memes.description: Sorrow as a pervasive theme in the Fifty-One Tales — personal grief, cosmic mourning, ritual lament, and the sadness inherent in mortal existence.

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: The soul in Fifty-One Tales as the enduring essence that persists beyond death — the grey ponderer within, the company of the dead, and the intangible element that Dunsany contrasts with the merely physical.

memes.label: Spring In Town
memes.description: A single-line tale evoking the arrival of spring in an urban setting — a title that holds within it the entire transformation of a city touched by the returning season.

memes.label: Taking Up Picadilly
memes.description: Walking down Picadilly, the narrator encounters workmen who are literally dismantling the famous London street — and are not what they appear to be.

memes.label: Taking Up Picadilly
memes.description: "Taking Up Picadilly" is a tale in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales that depicts the event of a London street being physically removed — an act of surreal urban transformation.

relations.label: The Trouble in Leafy Green Street

memes.label: The Angel
memes.description: A supernatural being who appears in two tales, walking by the sea with a worm and building an expansion to Hell near a factory city.

memes.label: The Assignation
memes.description: A brief allegorical tale in which Fame ignores the poet's crafted chaplets of song in favor of worthless garlands thrown by boisterous citizens.

memes.label: The Assignation
memes.description: The Assignation is the central event of Dunsany's tale of the same name — a poet's summoned meeting with Fame in a golden city, where artistic devotion and the price of recognition are tested.

memes.label: The Blackbird
memes.description: A singing blackbird who explains to the Poet the secret of its art — that it once opened its beak in wonder at a "wonderful she-blackbird" and has sung ever since in memory of that moment.

memes.label: The City
memes.description: A traveler in a desert watches poets and artists quietly abandon a city — and a thousand years later finds nothing of it among the weeds.

memes.label: The Demagogue
memes.description: A political figure in Dunsany's "The Demagogue and the Demi-monde" who, after death, is asked to account for his public life — and answers that he "stood for those principles" that the public demanded.

memes.label: The Demagogue and the Demi-monde
memes.description: A demagogue and a demi-mondaine arrive together at the gate of Paradise; the Saint admits the one who was honest about wanting money, and turns away the one who wrapped self-interest in political principle.

memes.label: The Demi-mondaine
memes.description: A woman of the demi-monde who, after death, finds herself at the gate of Paradise alongside a demagogue — and confesses with startling honesty that her life was driven by the need for money, not virtue.

memes.label: The Devil
memes.description: A supernatural antagonist who appears in a Puritan's dream, claiming credit for the suppression of joy and dance, and revealing a sardonic contempt for human happiness.

memes.label: The Dream of King Karna-Vootra
memes.description: King Karna-Vootra recounts a dream in which he saw the queenly Vava-Nyria and invited her to walk with him through the gardens and secret paths of Istrakhan, a city shut in by mountains older than the moon.

memes.label: The Dream of King Karna-Vootra
memes.description: The Dream of King Karna-Vootra is the prophetic vision that foretells an invasion — the central event of Dunsany's tale, in which a king dreams of an enemy force and must act on what he has seen.

memes.label: The End of the World
memes.description: The End of the World as imagined across multiple tales in Fifty-One Tales — not a single narrated event but a recurring horizon against which Dunsany measures the significance of all lesser endings.

memes.label: The Field
memes.description: An allegorical space in several tales representing pastoral life, the domain of children and song, and the landscape that will return when civilization passes.

memes.label: The Flood of the Centuries
memes.description: The Flood of the Centuries is the eschatological event described in "The Latest Thing" — when enough centuries have melted on the hills of Fate, the river of Time floods and sweeps away the accumulated filth of civilization.

memes.label: The Food of Death
memes.description: Death falls ill and is nursed back to strength by the very products of modern civilization — adulterated bread, tinned meat, and patent medicines.

memes.label: The Giant Poppy
memes.description: The narrator returns to childhood hills to find the woods and fairies gone, replaced by a giant poppy humming "Remember not" — and a poet-piper who plays an old tune to hold back the poppy's amnesiac advance.

memes.label: The God of Rainy Cheerfulness
memes.description: A minor deity purchased from an idol-shop in Moleshill Street — a grey-stone figurine that a London woman prays to on wet days in Dunsany's tale "The Lonely Idol."

memes.label: The Golden Town
memes.description: The Golden Town is a mythical holy city in "The Messengers" — once beautiful with golden doors and pillars, but fallen into corruption, darkness, and trouble, to which the Muses insist on sending messengers none will believe.

memes.label: The Guest
memes.description: A young man in a London restaurant dines alone at a table laid for two, carrying on an elaborate monologue with an empty chair — whose occupant finally arrives with the coffee.

memes.label: The Hare
memes.description: A boastful hare who agrees to race a tortoise over five hundred yards, confident of victory until the tortoise's supporters convince him the race is meaningless.

memes.label: The Hen
memes.description: A satirical fable in which a farmyard hen, inspired by the swallows' annual migration, announces her own journey South — only to reach the high road and return with a traveller's tale that the poultry-yard finds deeply impressive.

relations.label: The True History of the Hare and the Tortoise

memes.label: The Immortals
memes.description: In Dunsany's "Alone the Immortals," the dead years are revealed as colossal immortal beings seated beyond the moon — creators of all gods and kings, before whom Time itself kneels in vassalage.

memes.label: The Latest Thing
memes.description: An unclean-feeder crouches by the river of Time, greedily snatching whatever foul things float down from the cities, while a poet watches with distant eyes — and warns that the river will one day terribly flood.

memes.label: The Little City
memes.description: A traveller on a train glimpses a little city in a valley, its smoke turned golden by the sun, and reflects on the ephemerality of human settlements against the enduring backdrop of mountains and sea.

memes.label: The Lonely Idol
memes.description: "The narrator takes pity on a forgotten stone idol — a \"little swine-faced\" god to whom no one prays — and offers it a prayer not for divine favour but as an elegy for all that fades: youth, beauty, song, memory, and the gardens of childhood."

memes.label: The Lonely Idol
memes.description: A little swine-faced idol of outlandish stone — a forgotten god to whom no one prayed — who receives the narrator's compassionate prayer in "The Lonely Idol."

memes.label: The Man With the Golden Ear-rings
memes.description: The narrator encounters a dark-skinned sailor at the docks — the last of the pirates — who cannot die because he has sinned too deeply on the Spanish seas.

memes.label: The Messengers
memes.description: A hare-hunter wandering near Parnassus hears the Muses ask him to carry a message to the Golden Town — and when he protests his unworthiness, they reply that he is exactly the messenger they need.

memes.label: The Mist
memes.description: When the mist comes up weeping into the Downs, the narrator asks a prophet why — and learns that the mist is a company of dead souls who never saw the Downs in life and now mourn what they missed.

memes.label: The Muses
memes.description: The divine patrons of the arts, appearing in Dunsany's tales as remote beings who send messengers from Parnassus to the Golden Town, only to see each messenger rejected and stoned.

memes.label: The Poet
memes.description: A recurring figure in several of Lord Dunsany's tales, representing the artist striving for recognition — most prominently in "The Assignation," where he seeks Fame's approval with his chaplets of song.

memes.label: The Prayer of the Flower
memes.description: Flowers cry out on the West wind against the encroaching city and the loss of the woods — and from far Arcady, Pan answers them with a promise of patience and an assurance that these things are not for long.

memes.label: The Prayer of the Flower
memes.description: The Prayer of the Flower is the central event of Dunsany's tale — a wild flower prays to Nature, its mother, in a petition that is at once humble and world-shattering.

memes.label: The Puritan
memes.description: A pious man in Dunsany's fable "The True History of the Hare and the Tortoise" who holds dancing to be sinful — and whose rigid morality is used by the Hare and Tortoise to deceive the world and claim a victory they never earned.

relations.label: The True History of the Hare and the Tortoise

memes.label: The Raft-Builders
memes.description: A meta-literary meditation in which Dunsany compares all writers to sailors hastily building rafts upon doomed ships — small vessels of thought that may, or may not, float briefly upon the sea of Oblivion before sinking into the deeps with Babylon, Nineveh, and Tyre.

memes.label: The Return of Song
memes.description: The Return of Song is the subject of a tale in Dunsany's collection — an event of artistic resurgence after a period of silence, representing the cyclical nature of creative inspiration.

memes.label: The Return of the Exiles
memes.description: The Return of the Exiles is the subject of a tale in Dunsany's collection — an event of homecoming after long exile, exploring themes of belonging, change, and the impossibility of true return.

relations.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana

memes.label: The Reward
memes.description: In a dream, the narrator visits Hell and finds an angel building a new wing — not for ancient sins, but for the inventors of a "new cheap yeast" whose advertising will torment the damned for ever.

memes.label: The Saint
memes.description: The guardian at the gate of Paradise in Dunsany's "The Demagogue and the Demi-monde," who questions the newly dead about their earthly lives — and renders judgment with weary regret rather than righteous severity.

memes.label: The Song of the Blackbird
memes.description: A poet asks a blackbird how he creates his song, and the blackbird recounts — in rambling, digressive, utterly unsentimental detail — the spring day and the she-blackbird that inspired it, revealing the gulf between the experience of inspiration and its polished result.

memes.label: The Songless Country
memes.description: A poet visits a country that has no songs, makes some for its people, and is rebuffed with the argument that they have no time for "nonsense" in an age of modern commerce.

memes.label: The Sphinx
memes.description: A personified ancient monument appearing in two tales by Lord Dunsany — at Giza, where her painted face captivates Time himself, and in Thebes, Massachusetts, where a modern counterpart is discovered in an unlikely American setting.

memes.label: The Sphinx at Giza
memes.description: A prose meditation in which the narrator contemplates the Sphinx at Giza, whose painted face has beguiled Time himself — and imagines what humanity might do if the Sphinx could be persuaded to betray Time's secret.

memes.label: The Sphinx in Thebes (Massachusetts)
memes.description: A wealthy woman in a steel-built city demands a live sphinx. Her agents capture one from a remote desert — but when the sphinx asks her a riddle she cannot answer, the ancient equation of mystery and mortality reasserts itself in modern America.

memes.label: The Storm
memes.description: Onlookers dismiss a small, strange-looking ship caught in a storm — but years later it returns as a galleon laden with gold and jewels, having had the gods on board all along.

relations.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana

memes.label: The Storm
memes.description: "The Storm" is a tale in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales that narrates a powerful storm event — a force of nature disrupting the human world.

memes.label: The Three Tall Sons
memes.description: At the apex of his civilization, Man sits enthroned above his ultimate city while his three tall sons — Famine, Pestilence, and War — wait patiently in the outer darkness for their inevitable return.

memes.label: The Tomb of Pan
memes.description: The enlightened people build a marble tomb for dead Pan, carving his humbled image with angels' feet on his neck — but as the afterglow gleams on the monument, a few weep apart, mourning not the god's wickedness but his death.

memes.label: The Tortoise
memes.description: A tortoise who agrees to race a hare over five hundred yards and, through the persuasive arguments of his supporters, convinces the hare to abandon the contest entirely.

memes.label: The Tradesman
memes.description: A furniture maker and merchant in "Time and the Tradesman" who is confronted by Time with the impermanence of all crafted things.

memes.label: The Trouble in Leafy Green Street
memes.description: A woman buys a grey-stone god from a shop in Moleshill Street to worship on rainy days — and brings down on her suburban home "that doom of which all men speak."
aliases: The Trouble in Leafy Green Street (tale)

memes.label: The True History of the Hare and the Tortoise
memes.description: A single-line tale whose title subverts Aesop's classic fable — promising a "true history" to overturn the legendary race between the hare and the tortoise, yet the tale itself consists of nothing but this title.

memes.label: The Unpasturable Fields
memes.description: A dialogue between the ancient mountains and the passing clouds, in which the mountains boast of their permanence only to be reminded that Pegasus prances upon the clouds' unpasturable fields.

memes.label: The Workman
memes.description: A tale in which a narrator witnesses a workman fall to his death — only to be visited that night by the dead man's laughing ghost, who mocks the futility of all human labour in the face of eternity.

memes.label: The Worm
memes.description: The humblest of earthly creatures, dwelling in darkness and soil, who encounters an angel in Lord Dunsany's brief allegorical tale, embodying the dignity of the small within the cosmic order.

memes.label: The Worm and the Angel
memes.description: A brief, allegorical tale in which a humble worm encounters an angel, exploring the vast distance between the lowliest earthly creature and the heavenly — and the unexpected dignity of the small.

memes.label: Thebes
memes.description: Thebes appears in Dunsany's Fifty-One Tales both as the ancient Egyptian city and, satirically, as Thebes, Massachusetts — a symbol of Time's destructive power and the mockery of ancient grandeur.

memes.label: Thlunrana
memes.description: Thlunrana, the secret rocky citadel in Dunsany's tale "How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana" — a doomed fortress-city whose fall was prophesied and whose terror haunted the surrounding lands.

relations.label: How the Enemy Came to Thlunrana

memes.label: Time and the Tradesman
memes.description: An allegorical fable in which Time enters a furniture shop and teaches a tradesman about the impermanence of crafted things and mortal life.

memes.label: Twilight
memes.description: Twilight in Fifty-One Tales as the liminal hour between day and night, vision and obscurity — the moment when Dunsany's tales most often reveal their mysteries, transformations, and quiet revelations.

memes.label: Tyre
memes.description: The ancient Phoenician city, appearing in the Fifty-One Tales as a sunken wreck on Oblivion's sea and as a name in Time's catalogue of destructions.

memes.label: What We Have Come To
memes.description: An advertiser weeps at the sight of cathedral spires — not from awe but from regret that they are not an advertisement for Beefo, a moment of savage satire on the commercial soul's inability to see beauty except as a missed marketing opportunity.

memes.label: Wind
memes.description: Wind as a recurring symbol in the Fifty-One Tales — a force of change, a messenger of seasons, a character in its own right, and an agent of fate.

memes.label: Wind and Fog
memes.description: A brief fable in which the boastful North Wind confronts the silent Fog, who offers no reply — only to reveal, when night falls, a dreadful catalogue of the ships he has destroyed across the ages.

memes.label: Wonder
memes.description: Wonder is a recurring sensibility in Fifty-One Tales — the astonished response to beauty, the extraordinary, and the sublime that lies beyond ordinary comprehension.

memes.label: The Death of Pan
memes.description: Travellers from London enter Arcady and lament the death of the god Pan — until Arcadian maidens laugh at his prone form and the old god springs back to life, sending echoes of pursuit through the hills.

memes.label: The Return of Song
memes.description: In a dream-vision of Valhalla, the narrator watches gods observe the death of a distant world — and the swans returning song to the heavens, while larks descend to seed a new one with music.

memes.label: Index of Works
memes.description: Complete index of all 51 tales from Fifty-One Tales by Lord Dunsany, grouped by section.

memes.label: Zoth
memes.description: The holy river in the Demagogue's vision, whose banks carry the rumour of beauty from Sendara to the plains-people.
